metadata.title: Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using Topic Modelling and Lexicon-Based Approaches

abstract: It is well recognized that ISIS have effectively utilized several channels to disseminate their propaganda. Producing a considerable amount of unstructured data, the Al-Hayat Media Centre had a cornerstone role in the implementation of the propaganda which targeted audiences of the Western world. Although there was a considerable effort by researchers to analyse the ISIS's online propaganda, there are very few studies which try to relate the above-mentioned material with the emotions associated with it. In this paper, we study the influence of all known issues of the Islamic State magazines, published by the Al-Hayat Media Centre, used to disseminate propaganda, and introduced ideas to audiences outside Iraq and Syria. We perform a series of natural language processing techniques, such as Emotion and Polarity Analysis using a lexicon-based approach and topic modelling with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA). We extract deep insights from the terrorist corpus, including the emotions that are dominant in the text, the combination of emotions (patterns) that the magazine authors attempt to evoke to the reader, the main topics of discussions and the overall 'feel' and theming of the propaganda. The combination of lexiconbased emotion analysis with the topic modelling further achieves a reduction in the 'emotion noise' that can be observed when applying the lexicon-based approach only, making clear which the dominant emotions are. The valuable findings discussed in this paper contribute to the existing Information Warfare literature as they provide a whole new perspective in the subject of ISIS's propaganda. The method also aims to open an interdisciplinary discussion, with applications in politics, marketing, etc.

paragraphs: For a political organization, the successful diffusion of its ideology is of great importance. During the last decades, political organizations have extensively utilized ICT methods and tools, as those provide the perfect means for a broad, easy and fast dispersion of ideas (Oates, 2003). Many researchers argue that the same rule applies to military and terrorist organizations (Bloom & Daymon, 2018;Ingram, 2017;Prucha, 2016;Svete, 2008;Jefferson, 2007).

In relation to ISIS, those researchers support that, the Islamic State of Iraq and al-Sham (briefly, ISIS), dedicated considerable amount of human and material resources in an effort to acquire competence in ICT. The main purpose of this decision was the plan to diffuse the ISIS's propaganda, which, in turn, would ensure continuous flows of supporters and active members (Bloom & Daymon, 2018;Cohen-Almagor, 2012;Ferrara, 2017). Carafella et al. (2019) argue, 'As a consequence of those strategic choices, the ISIS is not defeated despite the loss of the territory. It is stronger today than its predecessor Al Qaeda in Iraq (AQI) was in 2011'.

It is widely recognized by antiterrorism specialists that ISIS had great expectations from the successful implementation of the Information Warfare, considering it as a cornerstone target-goal of its strategic design (Bloom & Daymon, 2018;Gambhir, 2016;Ingram, 2017;Winter, 2015). Winter (2015) asserted, 'the Islamic State has captured the imagination of the international media like no terrorist group before it, and exhibited a branding strategy, delivered by the meticulously planned production of propaganda and cultivation of a uniquely challenging network of disseminators'. Sharing the same way of thought, Gambhir (2016) states that 'The ISIS's Information operation campaign has supported multiple objectives including control over territory, coercion of populations and recruitment'. Ingram (2017) states that 'It is essential to position ISIS's online propaganda efforts into the context of not just its propaganda strategy but as a broader politico-military campaign'. In this direction, Winter (2015) categorizes the audience of this propaganda into the following groups: Supporters and engaged individuals, Enemies or potential opponents, Uninvolved publics and Islamic State's disseminators, proselytizers and enlisters.

According to several thinkers, 'the ISIS's propaganda could be decomposed into a set of four to six narratives' (Bloom & Daymon, 2018;Winter, 2015). The above-mentioned narratives aim to trigger and amplify the pre-propaganda background of the individual in relation to the following components: Brutality, Mercy, Victimhood, War, Belonging and Utopianism. The success of this propaganda could be considered as a confirmation of the validity of Ellul's (1965) thoughts, according to which 'the successful propaganda should aim to trigger and realize the compounding and crystallizing of pre-existing ideas in focused groups'.

We believe that the detailed analysis of the reasons that have ensured the successful diffusion of ISIS propaganda and the recovery of the parameters that determine this success constitute an interesting research problem.
The research questions that have driven this research are the following:

a. Is it possible to gain deeper insights of the emotions the propaganda authors are trying to evoke to the reader? How are those insights better than polarity analysis alone? b. Can we identify and extract these emotions and patterns from the propaganda corpus? c. Are there any differences in the evoked emotions or patterns, between magazines targeting different audiences (English, French, Russian and Turkish)? If so, in what manner are the results different? d. What are the topics of discussion?

In order to give answers to those research questions, we adopted a lexicon-based and a machine learning approach, focussing on the analysis of unstructured data (i.e. text analysis and opinion mining, see also Junger and Gartner (2021)). Using these techniques, all known ISIS magazines that were published throughout the years are analysed.

The structure of the paper is the following. In Section "Understanding the ISIS Propaganda", a review of the research and reports on the ISIS propaganda is presented. The Section "Methodological Approach" exploits the research methodological framework and the details about data collection, data preparation and preprocessing, statistical analysis and text analysis. The results of the analysis are discussed in Section "Results" followed by the conclusions.

paragraphs: There have been multiple attempts to define propaganda throughout the decades. However, the scientific community does not seem to have come to a universal agreement (Bernays, 1972;Biddle, 1931;Cunningham, 2002;Henderson, 1943;Jowett and O'Donnell, 2019;Koppang, 2009;Lieberman, 2017;Walton, 1996). There is some wider alignment in the elements of propaganda though. For example, some experts agree on the point that propaganda does not necessarily consist of lies (Becker, 1949;Bernays, 1972;Cunningham, 1992Cunningham, , 2002) ) or the element of deception (Hutchinson, 2006;Macdonald, 2007;Milton, 2020;Shulsky, 2000). One such element that appears frequently in the literature for the definition of propaganda is 'emotions'. The management of emotions of a target audience is always a vital process within the methodology and practice of propaganda (Biddle, 1931;Cunningham, 2002;Jowett and O'Donnell, 2019;Lieberman, 2017;Walton, 1996). Despite its importance though, emotion has not been examined in this context extensively, and through the scope of recent technological advancements like the ones in the field of machine learning.

As stated earlier, ISIS has extensively focused on its propaganda, using a variety of means (i.e. video, photos, textual content and audio) to spread information globally, through the exploitation of technology and the internet (Bloom & Daymon, 2018;Gambhir, 2016). This is a result of a highly structured organization with a wide communications' wing, operating by military trained media specialists (ISIS invests both in the military training and media training of its troopers), under an effective leadership (Gambhir, 2016). In this way, ISIS has managed to create a solid and recognizable brand with a global scale, outreach and impact. This is of course, also validated by (and not limited to) the supporters of ISIS worldwide, the foreign jihadists and the higher number of recruitments, compared to the recruiting efforts inside Syria and Iraq (McCabe, 2016). Al-Tamimi (2015) leaked a number of ISIS documents to the Guardian news agency. Those documents revealed a considerable amount of the ISIS's structure (Al-Tamimi, 2015;Malik, 2015). A blueprint of the ISIS's Media wing can be also found in Gambhir (2016, p.21). As it is demonstrated in the aforementioned page, all primary media houses operate under the supervision and leadership of the 'Central Media Diwan'. Our attention is focused on Al-Hayat Media Centre, publisher of the magazines Dabiq, Rumiyah (Dabiq's successor (Gambhir, 2016;Kinney, et al., 2018)), Istok, Dar al Islam and Konstantiniyye that are the main subject of this research paper. The branding methodology of ISIS is not discussed in this paper as extensive work has already been done on this subject (Gambhir, 2016;Kinney, et al., 2018;Impara, 2018;Ingram, 2017;Winter, 2015).

As already mentioned, there are five different series of magazines published by the Al-Hayat Media Centre. Dabiq and Rumiyah are the longest-standing ones among them. Table 1 presents some basic information related to each magazine series. The magazines follow the Islamic Calendar (month and year), to which a Gregorian equivalent is provided by approximation in parenthesis (Richards, 2012).

Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
paragraphs: The magazines were downloaded in PDF format from the public clearing house jihadology.net (Kinney, et al., 2018). Due to the fact that those magazines were primarily distributed through the deep web, we also downloaded the magazines from a different source (https://archive.org). We cross-validated the content from each source to ensure authenticity. Despite this collection of magazines being the only corpus officially issued by Al-Hayat Media Centre, there is a wealth of text data to explore. Table 2 provides an overview of content volume available in each magazine. The Articles column values correspond strictly to the number of articles that contain text data, within the issue. In other words, sections solely consisting of photographic reporting or infographics with no proper textual content are excluded.

It is worth mentioning that some magazine issues contain article sections that are not listed in the table of contents. In these cases, the extras are summed along with the rest of the articles. This creates a virtual discrepancy compared to the number derived by the table of contents that should be ignored. A complete break-down of article and page count can be found in Appendix G in the Supplementary material. Appendix B also contains the word counts of each magazine issue. While this dataset consists of a smaller number of articles compared to other datasets, it is still the entirety of all known Al-Hayat Media Centre publications in this format. In addition, this corpus is still larger in size than the one used by Kinney et al. (2018) in similar research, where the articles were limited to Dabiq, Rumiyah and Inspire (Al-Qaeda's periodical).

paragraphs: All PDF files were converted to text files using optical character recognition (OCR) software (Kinney, et al., 2018). Some minor cases where text failed to convert were footnotes of insignificant length and content, and infographics. The non-English texts from Dar al Islam, Istok and Konstantiniyye were translated to English prior to the analysis. This intervention is proven to not negatively impact the analysis process (Balahur & Turchi, 2012;Chatzakou, et al., 2017;Martin-Valdivia, et al., 2013). The translation process was implemented through Python, using the Google Translate API. We picked Google Translate as it has been used before, to successfully translate text prior to sentiment analysis (Balahur & Turchi, 2012;Chatzakou, et al., 2017).

sections.header_norm: latent dirichlet allocation lda topic modelling
paragraphs: To perform topic modelling, we used the Gensim library (Rehurek & Sojka, 2010). We loaded the output of the previous step and we examined the data using LDA, which is a generative probabilistic model for text corpora, based on a three-level hierarchical Bayesian model (Blei, et al., 2003). The reason for using LDA is based on the previous work and impactful results of Kinney et al. (2018) in a similar context (see also Hagen, 2018). LDA has also been used by Curiskis et al (2020) as a benchmark comparison with other topic modelling methods. Moreover, Zuorba et al (2017) proposed a framework for identifying sadness through social media, a method that combined sentiment analysis with topic modelling. Figure 2 shows the workflow segment relating to LDA that is fed the clean word lists per magazine issue, produced in Section Preprocessing.

sections.paper_id: Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
paragraphs: To identify and extract specific emotions, we used a lexicon-based approach. Specifically, we take advantage of EmoLex, a crowdsourced lexicon that relates words with primary emotions (Mohammad & Turney, 2010;Mohammad & Turney, 2013). It has been successfully used in previous studies (Chatzakou, et al., 2017;Zuorba, et al., 2017) and has a high word coverage, while it also includes earlier lexicons (Meiselman, 2016;Mohammad & Turney, 2013). Furthermore, the corpus used in this research is not restricted by character limitations like the ones present in social media posts that would encourage the use of abbreviations. There is also no use of informal language or emoticons (Harb, et al., 2020). Each word in Emolex is assigned 10 ratings. These ratings correspond to the eight primary human emotions according to Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions (Plutchik, 1980) a widely accepted model (Meiselman, 2016); and two more ratings representing the word's positive and negative polarity (Mohammad & Turney, 2010, 2013;Martin-Valdivia et al. 2013). A rating r can take values as follows:
r 2 f r j 0 ≤r ≤1 g

In this way, we attempt to identify the emotions at play, a task where polarity analysis alone fails to produce deep insights. However, we do include the polarity aspect, since it helps to demonstrate the superiority of emotions over polarity.

sections.paper_id: Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
paragraphs: For each magazine issue, we extracted the text and kept the cleaned lemmatized tokens, as explained in Sections Data Preparation and Preprocessing. The output of this work was exported into a list of lemmas. Each lemma was looked up against the lexicon and the associated emotions were retrieved where a match was found. Through this process, each document (magazine issue) was converted from a list of lemmas, into a list of emotion and polarity ratings. For words found in the lexicon, two arrays were returned. One 8-element array for emotion ratings (joy, trust, fear, surprise, sadness, disgust, anger and anticipation) and a 2element array containing polarity (positive and negative). The process is demonstrated in Figure 3. Note that words not found in the lexicon were excluded from the output. This was preferred from assigning approximate or zero ratings to non-matches, as such an approach would produce inaccuracies.

To find the aggregated emotions and polarities of each document, the ratings were summed together at the level of emotion and polarity. For example, the Joy ratings of every word from our output in Figure 3 were summed. Similarly, for the rest of the emotions and polarities.

Due to the different number of word count and the varying lexicon coverage for each document, the summed emotion and polarity ratings were difficult to compare between the documents. For this reason, we implemented a simple normalization approach. The emotion specific totals were summed to find the global amount of emotion in the document. In other words, the total of Joy was summed with the total of Trust, total of Fear, etc. Then, each total emotion was divided by this global. The same calculation was done for polarity. This way, all documents received their relative amounts of each emotion and polarity with the respect to their global.

To better demonstrate this process, we can consider the eight emotion ratings of each word, as an eight-dimensional vector. Similarly, polarity can be taken as a two-dimensional vector. Given the above, we can proceed with the following:
• The corpus C is the set containing all documents D. In other words, C ¼ fD 1 , D 2 , …,D m g with D i the document of index i of the corpus
• For the words in D i , we have
The algorithm performs:
Step 1: Creation of emotion and polarity vectors at document level
Step 2: Normalization of emotion and polarity vectors

) Table 3 presents an example where values from left to right represent in sequence the emotions of joy(1), trust(2), fear(3), surprise(4), sadness(5), disgust(6), anger(7) and anticipation(8); their respective r index is given in parenthesis. In a similar fashion, the calculation and normalization of polarity is performed. The same process is also repeated to calculate and normalize the emotions and polarity of the words extracted using LDA. This approach was chosen because it provides a comparative view of a specific emotion, against the sum of all emotions present in a given piece of text. Additionally, this allows comparisons between texts of different lengths without the need of padding. This is especially helpful when comparing the normalized emotion and polarity of the topic models, against the respective normalized vectors of each document.

sections.paper_id: Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
paragraphs: The initial step of the research was to conduct basic exploratory analysis, in order to gain an understanding of trends between the magazines. First, the word count and coverage were calculated and visualized. Coverage is the ratio of words matched with a word in Emolex, divided by the total number of words in the magazine issue, after preprocessing (word count). On average, a coverage of 65% was achieved with Emolex. English-speaking magazine Dabiq had a mean of 67% in coverage, while Rumiyah and the Russian-speaking Istok, both had 70% (detailed table in Appendix A). The two most interesting charts were for Dabiq and Rumiyah (targeting Englishspeaking audiences). For Dabiq, there is a clear upward trend in the word count per issue (Figure 4), throughout its history.

The last issue, published in July 2016, reached an all-time high with a bit over 50,000 words. This was followed by a U.S. airstrike in Syria, on August 2016, that killed Abu Muhammad al-Adnani, director of external operations and spokesman for ISIS. This made experts wonder whether ISIS's media operations would continue to function. Six days later, Rumiyah succeeded Dabiq (Gambhir, 2016), however, with what appears to be less content based on word count. Rumiyah never managed to surpass Dabiq's highest word count, and only achieved a max of 36,054 words with the 11 th issue.

Another important observation is the downward trend that started with Rumiyah's fourth issue, published in December 2016. There is a considerable decrease in word count (Figure 5) over the next issues until the 7 th periodical. It is worth noting that several serious conflicts are known to have taken place during December according to reports (Gambhir, 2016). The corresponding charts for all the magazines can be found in Appendix B Word Count and Coverage Charts.

sections.header_raw: Polarity Analysis at Magazine Issue Level
sections.header_norm: polarity analysis at magazine issue level
paragraphs: Contrary to initial expectations anticipating negative sentiment to be dominant in every magazine, both polarities are abundant and almost equal, with the positive sentiment ranking slightly higher almost in every issue. For this reason, instead of calculating an overall polarity (overall polarity = positive polaritynegative polarity) at this point, it was chosen to maintain both. The hypothesis behind this is that both sentiments can coexist within a piece of text, triggering different emotional reactions to the reader, interchangeably. In a different scenario where the overall polarity is calculated, the result would inaccurately appear as mostly neutral. In both cases, whether we have an overall polarity, or both polarities maintained separately, the methods fail to capture the exact emotional profile in the text. As a result, it is also unclear what are the emotions being evoked to the reader. Figure 6 shows the mean positive and mean negative of all issues per magazine. More information can be found in the Supplementary material.

sections.paper_id: Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
sections.header_raw: Emotion Analysis at Magazine Issue Level
sections.header_norm: emotion analysis at magazine issue level
paragraphs: One of the major findings of this project was the result of measuring the primary emotions of each individual issue, across all magazines. Looking at the overall picture first, the global mean is suggesting the existence of an emotion pattern present in all magazines (see Figure 7). A very interesting note is that the emotion of Trust appears to be dominant across the corpus. This is followed by Fear, Joy and then Sadness, Anger and Anticipation. Disgust and Surprise are less present. It is already obvious at this point that the amount of detail achieved by measuring the individual emotions is far superior than polarity. We are able to see the specific emotions and their intensity as this is derived from the text. We can also argue that this is the overall combination of emotions that acts as a catalyst for ISIS in:
• Them building a follower base,
• Their recruiting efforts and • The effectiveness of the above.

This is an important highlight, demonstrating the wealth of information, that is most of the times ignored in research projects and commercial applications alike. There is a lot to be understood both for the author(s) of a piece of text, as well as the reader(s), using this method. By drilling down to magazine level, a clear pattern, followed by all magazines, can be observed (see Figure 8).

It is apparent that all magazines emphasize on each emotion with similar strength. The maximum difference at emotion level always remains below 0.05 (or 5%). We believe that there is an obvious pattern, to which the Al-Hayat Media Centre always stays true. Furthermore, looking at each magazine issue itself and by visualizing the emotion values on an eight-dimensional radar plot, the polygonal shape formed by the vertices gives a new perspective of the emotion pattern. Figure 9 presents the radar plots of four different magazine issues. Even though the shape changes slightly in each instance, still an overall tendency to follow a standard emotional profile can be observed. This emotional profile of each magazine, never deviates significantly from the overall pattern presented above, suggesting no outlier document in terms of emotion intensities. Radar plots for all magazine issues can be found in the Supplementary material.

paragraphs: We trained the LDA model on our corpus and produced the topics 10 times (Kohavi, 1995;Bengio & Grandvalet, 2004). The number of topics was set to 12 and the number of features at 1600 (Kinney, et al., 2018). The algorithm returned consistent results in each iteration. In total, 68 unique words were extracted during this process. Those were (in no particular order):

'way', 'god', 'place ', 'woman', 'right', 'imam', 'fear', 'allahin', 'east', 'group', 'among', 'order', 'good', 'fact', 'crusader', 'come', 'except', 'islamic', 'see', 'without', 'land', 'claim', 'saddle', 'mujahidin', 'messenger', 'city', 'khilafah', 'even', 'muslim', 'people', 'go', 'know', 'give', 'take', 'islam', 'cent', 'lord', 'brother', 'law', 'call', 'army', 'soldier', 'like', 'become', 'jihad', 'many', 'believer', 'battle', 'prophet', 'report', 'plus', 'time', 'religion', 'born', 'make', 'fight', 'force', 'indeed', 'honor', 'towards', 'kill', 'allah', 'state', 'sure', 'son', 'day', 'war' and 'enemy'

We can already identify the words that play a major role in ISIS's brand. Also, we can argue that those findings confirm in a quantitative and structured manner the assertions made by (Winter, 2015). In this work, the 'six themes' of ISIS's brand can be broken down to (1) Brutality, (2) Mercy, (3) Victimhood, (4) War, (5) Belonging and (6) Utopianism. One could argue for example that words like 'war', 'jihad (to strive/exert/fight (meaning) from ‫ﺟ‬ ‫ﻬ‬ ‫ﺎ‬ ‫ﺩ‬ (jih ad) (Esposito, 2003))', 'army', 'battle', 'kill', 'fight', 'mujahidin (someone that has engaged in Jihad (meaning) from ‫ﻣ‬ ‫ﺠ‬ ‫ﺎ‬ ‫ﻫ‬ ‫ﺪ‬ ‫ﻳ‬ ‫ﻦ‬ (mujahidin) (Esposito, 2003))' and 'soldier' serve mainly War and Brutality. Similarly, words like 'brother' and 'son' could contribute to Belonging, while 'state', 'land', 'Islamic' (frequently appears as the bigram 'Islamic state') and 'khilafah' (Caliphate (meaning) from ‫ﺧ‬ ِ ‫ﻼ‬ ‫ﻓ‬ ‫ﺔ‬ (khil afah) (Esposito, 2003)) to Utopianism. A validation to this assumption comes from the context of the magazines themselves, as well as the section describing Belonging in (Winter, 2015), where in Al-Hayat Media Center's videos 'brothers from around the world are filmed with each other in parks having a good time, their faces a picture of serenity'.

sections.header_raw: Emotion Analysis on LDA Topic Words
sections.header_norm: emotion analysis on lda topic words
paragraphs: After extracting the topic words using LDA, these were mapped to their emotion ratings. The great outcome of this experiment was that the emotion patterns of each LDA iteration matched the emotion patterns of the magazines that we also saw in Section Emotion Analysis at Magazine Issue Level. This is a major finding as it indicates that the extracted topic words are the ones most responsible in forming the emotional pattern that we see. Further to this discovery, the emotion analysis of topic words seems to have produced a distilled version of the emotional pattern of the propaganda corpus. By extracting emotions only from these words, the 'emotional noise' from less relevant words decreased dramatically.

The common tendency of all magazine issues towards a specific emotion pattern was mentioned earlier. Slight deviations between them can be observed and are expected, since this approach took into account all token words within each issue. The consequence is that a nondiscriminatory selection of words produces noiseemotional noise in this case. To eliminate this effect and achieve a more crystalized result, the emotion analysis of the LDA topic words is proposed instead as it proved more robust in this study. Figure 10 shows the emotion profile of the 68 unique words, presented in Section LDA Findings (coverage 79% -54 words matched in EmoLex out of 68).

sections.paper_id: Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
paragraphs: To summarize, during this research, all known Al-Hayat Media Centre propaganda magazines were collected, preprocessed, translated and processed using natural language processing techniques. Their content was analysed, extracting emotion ratings and polarities. The main findings of this research are following:

1. Deeper insights were achieved with emotion analysis, compared to polarity analysis; 2. A specific emotion pattern was discovered throughout the corpus; 3. None of the magazine issues showed deviation in terms of emotions and their intensity when compared to the overall emotion pattern. This suggests a strict standard that the periodicals follow in terms of emotions they try to evoke to the reader, regardless of language and ethnicity (Istok was a Russian speaking periodical, targeting Russian audiences, Dabiq and Rumiya were written in English and were targeting a wider Englishspeaking audience, Dar-al-Islam was similarly targeting French-speaking groups and Konstantiniyye Turkish ones); 4. The primary emotions the propaganda attempts to evoke to the reader the most are remarkably interesting. The three top ones based on their emotion-specific corpus average are Trust, Fear and Joy (in order from high to low); 5. Given points 2, 3 and 4, there is an indication that, despite previous research showing evidence of ISIS speaking differently to audiences based on language and ethnicity (Pennington and Krona, 2019;Sparks, 2018), the core emotional patterns driving the propaganda always remain similar. This is a major finding as it poses a new research question: Can we identify propaganda based solely on emotional patterns and regardless of the wording used, the language in which the text is written, the cultural differences between the targeted ethnic and/or social groups, and even the context (e.g. domestic politics instead of international conflict)? 6. Using LDA topic modelling, topic words were extracted from the corpus. These words are highly representative of the 'six themes' of ISIS's brand and confirm the assertions made by Winter (Winter, 2015) in a quantitative and structured manner; 7. Upon analysing the emotions behind the LDA topic words, the same emotion pattern emerged, as with the overall emotional pattern of the corpus and the emotional profile of the magazines and 8. This method of analysing the emotions of the LDA topic words further reduced the 'emotional noise', offering a distilled view of the emotion pattern and highlighting the topic words that shape the propaganda.

The results of this approach contribute to the existing Information Warfare literature. Other areas of research can also greatly benefit from these findings, especially in works analysing political events and personalities, marketing and branding, company culture and audience behaviour management in general. Our evidence suggests that emotions are a core driver in propaganda and that they transcend words, language, culture and context. As such, it is worth exploring whether similar methods have been used or are being used in other areas, like politics, election campaigns, marketing, company culture and more. Also, at what degree they have been/are applied, and what impact they have. In addition, specifically in the context of warfare and terrorism (whether domestic or international), this research demonstrates a clear way of identifying the emotions that propaganda disseminators are trying to evoke to the reader. By understanding this aspect, nations can better prepare and defend themselves against foreign information attacks. Successful efforts could be through deconstruction of the propaganda narrative and minimization of its distribution, and the creation and dissemination of an internal counter-narrative. The process demonstrates how existing and reliable tools and techniques can be applied to such situations and how the findings give a new insightful perspective of the problem at hand. Their combination also amplifies the clarity of results and highlights the keywords that ultimately shape the desired emotional profile. To the best of our knowledge, there is no other research on this topic that produces such results and in this extent.

To the best of our knowledge, there is no other research on this topic that produces such results and in this extent.

sections.paper_id: Maragkos and Maravelakis - 2023 - Extracting Primary Emotions and Topics from the Al-Hayat Media Centre Magazine Publications, Using T
paragraphs: There are some areas of this research that allow for further improvement. These concern mainly language limitations. For example, throughout the propaganda corpus, the use of Arabic words with English characters is plenty. Such instances include words like Allah, Islam, jihad, khilafah and mujahidin. These are arguably important words, also according to the LDA results, and many of them contain complex meanings that cannot be easily mapped with emotions in Emolex. It is important to note that the same word can have different meanings for different groups of people. An example of that is the meaning of jihad, mentioned in Section LDA Findings. Another interesting approach would be to examine whether a higher word coverage would be possible, by using the multilingual version of Emolex, or by expanding the lexicon itself (Ghiassi & Lee, 2018;Fernández-Gavilanes, et al., 2018). The dataset used can be considered small compared to others; however, it is larger that the one used by (Kinney, et al., 2018), where only Dabiq, Rumiyah and Inspire (Al-Qaeda's magazine) were analysed. It is also the entirety of Al-Hayat Media Centre's publications in this format that still contains a wealth of unstructured textual data. Lastly, due to the nature of these magazines, such content is almost always limited and hard to find.

paragraphs: The next steps of our research include the improvement on the limitations discussed above, as well as the expansion of the dataset to investigate how emotion profiles can assist in the classification of documents (see, for example, Elnagar et al., 2020). Main focuses of our ongoing project are:
• The improvement of the natural language preprocessing algorithm used;
• The expansion of the dataset and comparison of propaganda articles against other content and • The improvement of the lexicon's coverage.
Above all, we hope this research helps fortify our societies against terror and enables us to recognize, better understand and counter-act on propaganda attempts in general.
